notes: Two no-op user turns in ONE live pi REPL session separated by a `/model <provider>/<id>` command that swaps the active model between them. Turn 1 runs on model A (pi's boot default, openai-codex/gpt-5.5 in this recording); the user issues `/model openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini` — pi's REPL resolves the exact canonical provider/id reference and switches DIRECTLY via session.setModel() (NO arrow-key picker), writing a model_change line to the rollout but producing NO assistant turn; turn 2 then runs on model B. Verified live: one rollout file, sequence model_change(A) → user → assistant(model=A,stop) → model_change(B) → user → assistant(model=B,stop). THE MODEL SWITCH ITSELF IS NOT EXPRESSIBLE IN THIS DSL: the events.jsonl lifecycle stream the validator reads carries no model_name field (validate Phase struct has phase/expected_state/kind/max_delay_ms/duration_at_least_ms/same_session_as/new_session/invariants and nothing model-related). So this spec asserts only the TWO-TURN LIFECYCLE SKELETON on ONE UUID rollout — ready→working→ready twice on the SAME session_id, no waiting ever, a single process_exited at teardown. The model A→B change (and that TotalTokens accumulates across both) is the metrics-level summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden (diffed by replay-fixtures: it pins model B as the latest-wins ModelName) plus the pi parser unit tests on message.model / model_change extraction — NOT this file. SINGLE UUID: a model switch does NOT create a new session, so there is exactly one rollout session_id and one process_exited (contrast session-reset / session-resume, which mint a fresh UUID). WHY interactive (not headless `pi --print`): a `/model` switch only exists inside the REPL and the scenario needs two turns; the pi interactive driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh) keeps the rollout REPL alive across both turns and kills the tmux session once at the end → a SINGLE process_exited, not two. WHY no wait_turn after the `/model` slash (and none after turn 2): the driver's `slash` step bumps the expected-turn counter like `send`, but `/model` produces a model_change line with NO assistant turn — so a wait_turn placed after it (with the counter over-counted by one) would hang; the recipe drives turn 2 with a generous trailing sleep instead (same compromise as the pi synchronous-slash-command recipe). C7 / SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch (it bridges the brief gap until the UUID rollout transcript file appears), so session_birth — which only asserts 'a session appears in ready within 2s of launch' — legitimately matches that pre-session (same shape as pi/multi-turn-conversation and pi/synchronous-slash-command). The TURN ARCS must land on the UUID rollout session: t1_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher picks the FIRST working in the whole recording, which is necessarily the rollout session (the pre-session never goes working); every later turn phase, the pid_bind, and the teardown then pin to t1_start's session via same_session_as so a co-occurring pre-session can never satisfy them. HOW runs differ: the concrete model values (boot default A + the authenticated switch target B) are environment-dependent (issue #440 — assert presence/change, not a hardcoded pair); exact assistant wording, per-turn token counts, rollout UUID, PID, and sub-second timing vary; pi may flush a single turn as MORE than one working→ready burst, so the spec asserts at least two arcs that reset cleanly and never enter waiting, not an exact arc count. Stable structurally: at least two ready→working→ready arcs on ONE rollout session_id, a single process_exited, no waiting, and a final ready state — plus (golden-level, not here) a non-empty summary.model_name equal to model B.

text: A session appears in ready within 2s of REPL launch
First prompt → working on model A (binds the rollout session; the pre-session never enters working)
First reply completes on model A → back to ready; state never entered waiting
After the /model switch, the second prompt → working again on the SAME rollout session, now on model B
Second reply completes on model B → back to ready; state never entered waiting
PID bound to the rollout session
REPL torn down once after the second turn → a single process_exited on the rollout session; state immediately before teardown is ready
